risk_patterns.remediation: Document the basis for the delay damages calculation at the time of contract drafting. Include financing costs, lost rental income, additional professional fees, and third-party liabilities. Apply a reasonable cap (typically 5-10% of contract sum).

risk_patterns.name: Building Safety Act Non-Compliance for Higher-Risk Buildings
risk_patterns.trigger: Higher-risk building project without Building Safety Act 2022 gateway compliance, golden thread requirements, or dutyholder appointments
risk_patterns.description: The Building Safety Act 2022 imposes stringent requirements on higher-risk buildings (at least 18m or 7+ storeys with 2+ residential units). Non-compliance can result in the Building Safety Regulator refusing to grant a completion certificate, prohibiting occupation, and taking enforcement action against dutyholders.
risk_patterns.impact: Inability to obtain completion certificate, prohibition on occupation, enforcement action by the Building Safety Regulator, extended liability periods of up to 30 years for defective products, and reputational damage.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Determine whether the project is a higher-risk building. Check for gateway compliance provisions (Gateways 1, 2, and 3). Verify dutyholder appointments (Client, Principal Designer, Principal Contractor). Check for golden thread of building information requirements. Verify mandatory occurrence reporting provisions.
risk_patterns.remediation: Include comprehensive Building Safety Act compliance provisions covering all three gateways, dutyholder responsibilities, golden thread requirements, and mandatory occurrence reporting. Ensure collateral warranties and limitation provisions reflect the extended liability periods.
risk_patterns.real_world_impact: The Grenfell Tower tragedy and subsequent inquiry were key drivers behind the Building Safety Act 2022, which fundamentally reformed building safety regulation for higher-risk buildings.

risk_patterns.name: Missing Collateral Warranty Requirements
risk_patterns.trigger: Construction contract does not require the contractor to provide collateral warranties to funders, purchasers, or tenants
risk_patterns.description: Without collateral warranties, third parties (funders, purchasers, and tenants) have no direct contractual relationship with the contractor and cannot bring claims for defective work or design. This can affect the marketability and fundability of the development, and may breach the employer's obligations to its funder.
risk_patterns.impact: Development may be unfundable or unsellable without collateral warranties in place. Third parties have no direct recourse against the contractor for defective work.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Check for collateral warranty obligations in the main contract. Verify that warranties are required in favour of funders, purchasers, and tenants. Check the required warranty form. Verify that subcontractor warranties are also required.
risk_patterns.remediation: Include a contractual obligation to provide collateral warranties in approved form to funders, purchasers, and tenants. Append the agreed warranty forms as schedules to the building contract. Require flow-down to subcontractors and consultants.

risk_patterns.name: Contractor Suspension Without Proper Notice
risk_patterns.trigger: Contractor purports to suspend works without giving the required 7 days' written notice under s.112 of the Construction Act
risk_patterns.description: A contractor who suspends performance without complying with the statutory notice requirements under s.112 of the Housing Grants, Construction and Regeneration Act 1996 may lose the statutory protection against termination and delay damages. Improper suspension may itself constitute a breach of contract.
risk_patterns.impact: Contractor loses statutory protection and may be liable for delay damages. Employer may treat improper suspension as a repudiatory breach and terminate the contract.
risk_patterns.detection_guidance: Monitor for suspension threats or actual suspension of works. Verify that the required 7-day written notice has been served. Check that the ground for suspension (non-payment after final date) is valid. Verify that no effective pay less notice was served.
risk_patterns.remediation: Ensure the contract clearly sets out the suspension procedure including the 7-day notice requirement. Train project managers to recognise and respond to suspension notices promptly. Establish internal procedures for serving timely pay less notices.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Retention percentage exceeds 5% of interim payment valuations
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Retention above 5% is above market standard and places disproportionate cash flow pressure on the contractor. Industry standard is 3-5%, with an increasing trend toward 3% or retention bonds in lieu of cash retention.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: 3-5% retention is market standard under JCT and NEC4 contracts. Public sector increasingly moving to 0% retention or retention bonds.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Counter with 3% retention or offer a retention bond from a reputable surety in lieu of cash retention. If 5% is insisted upon, require retention to be held in a designated trust account.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Design liability clause imposes a fitness for purpose obligation on the contractor or designer
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Fitness for purpose is a strict liability standard that is generally uninsurable under professional indemnity insurance policies. Accepting fitness for purpose creates a gap between the contractor's contractual liability and its insurance cover, exposing the contractor to uninsured claims.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Reasonable skill and care is the standard design liability obligation under JCT DB 2016 clause 2.17.1 and most standard form construction contracts. Fitness for purpose is the exception, not the norm.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Reject fitness for purpose and counter with reasonable skill and care consistent with a competent professional of the relevant discipline. If the employer insists, limit fitness for purpose liability to the level of available PI insurance and require the employer to accept the insurance market position.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Liquidated damages rate exceeds 0.5% of contract sum per week or aggregate cap exceeds 10% of contract sum
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Delay damages above 0.5% per week or with an aggregate cap above 10% of the contract sum may be disproportionate to the employer's likely loss and could be challenged as penal under the Cavendish Square v Makdessi [2015] test.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Typical delay damages range from 0.1% to 0.5% of contract sum per week, with an aggregate cap of 5-10% of contract sum. The rate should reflect the employer's genuine pre-estimate of loss.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Request the employer's calculation basis for the delay damages rate. Counter with a rate that reflects documented likely losses (financing costs, lost rental income, additional professional fees). Insist on a reasonable aggregate cap of 5% of contract sum.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Extension of time clause does not cover employer-caused delays or neutral events such as exceptionally adverse weather
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: An extension of time clause that does not cover employer-caused delays may trigger the prevention principle, rendering the liquidated damages clause unenforceable. This paradoxically disadvantages the employer by placing time at large.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: All standard form construction contracts (JCT, NEC4, FIDIC) include comprehensive extension of time provisions covering both employer risk events and neutral events.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Require the extension of time clause to cover all employer risk events (late information, variations, employer-caused access delays) and neutral events (exceptionally adverse weather, force majeure). Reference the JCT Relevant Events list as a benchmark.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: No performance bond required for a contract exceeding GBP 1 million in value
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: For contracts above GBP 1 million, the absence of a performance bond leaves the employer without financial security to fund completion by an alternative contractor if the original contractor defaults. The cost of completing works following default typically exceeds the original contract sum by 20-40%.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Performance bonds at 10% of contract value are standard for contracts above GBP 500,000-1,000,000. The ABI Model Form of Guarantee Bond is the industry standard conditional form.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Require a performance bond at 10% of contract value in conditional (ABI) form from a surety with a minimum A- credit rating. The bond should remain in force until expiry of the defects liability period.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Variation clause allows unlimited scope changes without a cap on cumulative variations or a right for the contractor to object
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: An unlimited variation power allows the employer to fundamentally change the scope of the works without the contractor's agreement, potentially transforming the project beyond what was priced. The contractor should have the right to object to variations that exceed a reasonable threshold.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Standard forms permit variations within reasonable limits. Some bespoke contracts cap cumulative variations at 15-25% of contract sum, beyond which the contractor's consent is required.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Include a threshold (15-20% of contract sum) beyond which the contractor may object to further variations. Require all variations to be instructed in writing before work commences. Ensure the valuation mechanism is fair and includes profit.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Contract uses a standard form (JCT, NEC4, or FIDIC) without heavily amended risk allocation, with fair extension of time provisions, reasonable delay damages, and standard retention at 3-5%
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Unamended or lightly amended standard form construction contracts represent decades of industry consensus on fair risk allocation. They provide tried and tested provisions that are well understood by the market and have established case law.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Standard form contracts (JCT 2016, NEC4, FIDIC 2017) with only project-specific amendments represent best practice in risk allocation for construction projects.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Accept. Verify that any bespoke amendments are reasonable and do not undermine the balanced risk allocation of the standard form. Focus negotiation on genuinely project-specific matters rather than seeking to shift standard form risk positions.

negotiation_intelligence.condition: Subcontract payment terms exceed 45 days from application or include pay-when-paid provisions
negotiation_intelligence.explanation: Payment terms exceeding 45 days in a subcontract create severe cash flow pressure on smaller subcontractors and may breach the Prompt Payment Code. Pay-when-paid clauses are ineffective under s.113 of the Construction Act but their inclusion signals an attempt to transfer payment risk unlawfully.
negotiation_intelligence.market_standard: Subcontractor payment within 30 days of application is industry best practice. The Prompt Payment Code requires 95% of invoices to be paid within 60 days, with a target of 30 days.
negotiation_intelligence.suggested_response: Insist on payment within 30 days of application. Remove any pay-when-paid provisions as they are ineffective under the Construction Act and create legal risk. Reference the Prompt Payment Code and government procurement requirements.

clause_interactions.description: Retention deductions reduce the amounts actually received by the contractor under interim payment certificates, creating a cash flow impact that accumulates over the project duration.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the retention percentage is market standard (3-5%). Check whether a retention bond alternative is available. Ensure retention release dates align with practical completion and defects liability period expiry.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Excessive retention combined with standard payment terms creates disproportionate cash flow pressure on the contractor, potentially affecting the contractor's ability to fund the works.

clause_interactions.description: A valid extension of time mechanism is a prerequisite for enforceable liquidated damages. Without it, the prevention principle may render delay damages unenforceable where the employer causes any delay.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the extension of time clause covers all employer risk events and neutral events. Cross-reference the delay events against the JCT Relevant Events or NEC4 compensation events as a completeness check.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: If the extension of time clause does not cover employer-caused delays, time becomes at large and the employer loses the right to levy liquidated damages entirely.

clause_interactions.description: Design liability obligations are only meaningful if backed by professional indemnity insurance sufficient to fund claims. The design liability standard must be consistent with what PI insurers will cover.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Verify that the design liability standard (reasonable skill and care vs fitness for purpose) is insurable under the required PI policy. Confirm PI insurance coverage amounts are proportionate to the design risk. Check that the PI maintenance period aligns with the limitation period.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: A fitness for purpose design liability with a reasonable skill and care PI policy creates an insurance gap, leaving the designer personally exposed to the difference between the two standards.

clause_interactions.description: Back-to-back subcontract provisions that make subcontractor payment conditional upon main contract certification or payment may conflict with the statutory prohibition on pay-when-paid clauses under s.113 of the Construction Act.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Review back-to-back payment provisions carefully. Ensure that subcontractor payment is not conditional upon the main contractor receiving payment from the employer. Verify that the subcontract has an independent payment mechanism compliant with the Construction Act.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: Pay-when-paid provisions within back-to-back arrangements will be struck down by operation of law, and the Scheme for Construction Contracts will be implied to fill the gap, potentially on less favourable terms.

clause_interactions.description: Collateral warranties and step-in rights work together to protect third-party interests. The collateral warranty provides a direct cause of action for defective work, while step-in rights provide the mechanism for a funder to preserve the building contract upon employer default.
clause_interactions.review_guidance: Ensure that step-in rights are included in funder collateral warranties. Verify that the step-in notice period aligns with the funder's security enforcement timeline. Check that the contractor's obligations upon step-in are clearly defined.
clause_interactions.risk_if_misaligned: A collateral warranty without step-in rights leaves the funder unable to preserve the building contract upon employer default, potentially losing the value of work completed and creating project abandonment risk.
